texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Climate change resulting from the presence of greenhouse gases (GHGs) is becoming a serious issue of the 21st century. Carbon dioxide (CO2) is the most important GHG because an increase in CO2 concentration severely disrupts the balance of incoming and outgoing energy in the Earth's atmosphere (Rackley, 2010). In this scenario, carbon capture and storage (CCS) techniques have become one of the most important research topics, and many related projects have been started worldwide. International Energy Agency (IEA) has reported that massive amounts of flue gases from electric power plants are one of the main sources of CO2 emissions (IEA, 2007). Therefore, it is essential to remove CO2 from the flue gas stream of electric power plants by post-combustion capture to prevent global warming.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide (CO2) recovery by vacuum stripping technology using membranes was studied as an alternative to the desorption process at low temperatures (below 120 ◦ C) in conventional aqueous amine absorption process. Composite membranes were prepared by coating hydrophobic silicalite-filled polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) layers on polyethylene (PE) porous supports and used as new membrane strippers for CO2 recovery to prevent the typical pore wetting problem of hydrophobic porous membranes. Aqueous 30 wt% solutions of monoethanolamine (MEA), diethanolamine (DEA), and triethanolamine (TEA) were used as absorbents in the desorption process. CO2 fluxes were measured under various operation conditions, such as different vacuum pressures, stripping temperatures, CO2 loadings, types of amine solutions and operation time. CO2 stripping fluxes increased with increasing temperature and CO2 loading of amine solutions as well as with decreasing stripping pressures because of the enhanced upstream and downstream driving forces. CO2 stripping fluxes were much higher in the TEA and DEA solutions than in the MEA solutions. The thin-film silicalite-filled PDMS composite membranes showed excellent long-term stability in the vacuum stripping process when compared with porous polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membranes.

This process consists of absorption and desorption processes of CO2, which require various apparatus such as a packed tower, spray tower, venture scrubber, and bubble column. The amine absorption process involves some problems that are yet to be improved, such as low surface area per unit contact volume and long contact time between liquid-gas interfaces. A membrane contactor is potentially an alternative key technology to overcome the problems of the conventional amine absorption process using packed columns with low contact area per volume. The amine absorption process has also another important problem related to the energy efficiency of the CO2 stripping process; much of the energy (i.e., approximately 70%) is consumed during desorption in the amine absorption process, because high temperatures of about 120 ◦ C are generally required for CO2 stripping (Lee et al., 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Desorption based on the vacuum stripping method may offer an alternative process for reducing the energy consumption in aqueous amine process, because desorption can be carried out at low temperatures, below 100 ◦ C, for CO2 stripping. In this case, the energy demand is only electricity for the vacuum pump if a low grade heat existing in a power plant can be provided to the vacuum stripping process. Also, low temperature operation can prevent the thermal degradation of an amine solution.

texts.orig: However, to the best of our knowledge, few studies on CO2 desorption by of aqueous amine absorption process have been conducted so far (Okabe et al., 2008). When considering the membrane contactors, the main factors to be taken into account when using them for desorption are pore wetting, chemical stability, and the thermal stability of the membrane. Pore wetting is the most important problem that prevents the long-term use of the membrane contactor. Even very hydrophobic PTFE, which is sometimes recommended as the suitable membrane contactor for gas stripping, may not guarantee long-term stability in the membrane stripping process with hot amine solutions (Fang et al., 2009). According to a study on membrane vacuum regeneration (Fang et al., 2009), it is reported that some novel membranes which have the stronger hydrophobicity, the better thermal and chemical stabilities must be developed, in order to ensure the long-term stable operation of membrane vacuum regeneration process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, a preliminary study on CO2 regeneration behaviors in aqueous alkanolamine solutions using small-area thin-film composite membranes was done to check the feasibility of utilizing a thin film composite membrane in a membrane vacuum regeneration process. Hydrophobic thin-film silicalite-filled polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) composite membranes as a membrane contactor have been suggested; these dense composite membranes have better resistance to wetting than porous hydrophobic membranes in the vacuum stripping process of CO2 from aqueous alkanolamine solutions at high temperatures. Hydrophobic silicalite-filled PDMS was selected as a thin coating layer on porous polyethylene (PE) supports to give the composite membranes with low membrane resistance for vacuum stripping. Silicalite is known to have a MFI structure with same crystal structure as zeolite (Ahn et al., 2009), and it is originally very hydrophobic molecular sieves with nana-sized pores capable of highest CO2 adsorbing ability among various gases (Golden and Sircar, 1994). texts.orig: In a vacuum stripping membrane contactor, it is important to control the operating vacuum pressure and amine solution temperature as well as amine types, because the CO2 stripping fluxes depend on the driving force arising from the difference of CO2 partial pressures in aqueous amine feed side and vacuum permeate side divided by the membrane contactor. The CO2 partial pressure in the aqueous phase has to be higher than in the vacuum phase. As shown in Table 1, the partial pressure of CO2 in the aqueous amine solution phase increases with increasing temperature of the solution. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PDMS coating materials with hydrophobic silicalites were purchased from GE Bayer company (trade name: LSR 2630, Germany, silicalite content: 30 wt%, particle size: 0.2-1 𝛍 m). The thin-film composite membranes were fabricated by dissolving 10 wt% silicalite-filled PDMS in hexane and coating the solution on a porous PE support layer (pore size: 0.2 𝛍 m, thickness: 25 𝛍 m, porosity: 45%; SK chemical, Korea) with an automated coater (Comate TM -3000, Kipae, Korea), controlling the coating thickness and curing temperature. PTFE porous membrane (pore size: 0.45 𝛍 m, thickness: 80 𝛍 m; Sumitomo Electric Ind., Ltd., Japan) was used as a reference contactor material with the PDMS coated composite membrane. The area of the composite membrane and PTFE membranes was 17.35 cm 2 . MEA (99%), DEA (99%), and TEA (98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further treatment. Table 2 lists the physical properties of three amine absorbents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 30 wt% aqueous amine solutions were filled in the CO2 absorber. Then, pure CO2 gas (99.9%, Daesung Industrial Gas, Korea) was saturated to give the desired CO2 loading by a bubbling method. This rich (CO2-containing) amine solution was analyzed by titrator (Titrino 848 plus, Metrohm, Swiss) to check the CO2 loading and excess quantity of aqueous amine solutions were used to prevent CO2 loading change in the absorber during regeneration experiments. The solution was circulated by a gear pump (EW-75211-10, Cole-Parmer, USA) that could control the liquid flow rate. The liquid flow rate was kept at 500 mL/min to maintain turbulent flow in the membrane cell and to prevent the change of CO2 loading value in the amine solution in the membrane cell during the vacuum stripping experiment. The temperature of the rich amine solutions was elevated by passing them through two thermostats (C-DWB10, CPT Inc., Korea) to give the desired CO2 stripping temperatures in Table 3. texts.orig: 4.1. Preparation of thin-film silicalite-filled PDMS composite membranes
texts.text: 4.1. Preparation of thin-film silicalite-filled PDMS composite membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dense composite membranes with a hydrophobic silicalitefilled PDMS thin layer coated onto a PE support were fabricated by a solvent (hexane) casting method using the automated coater. A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Vega II LSU, TESCAN, Czech) image of the porous PE support layer and a magnification of its pores are shown in Fig. 2(a). Fig. 2(b) presents the SEM image of the PDMS dense layer, showing that the PDMS layer is defect-free and without pores. Fig. 2(c) shows the SEM images of the cross-section of PDMS-coated composite membrane, where the thickness of the coating layer is approximately 5 𝛍 m. These SEM images confirm that the composite membranes with dense silicalite-filled PDMS thin layer were prepared successfully.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Wetting resistance of thin-film silicate-filled PDMS composite membranes
texts.text: 4.2. Wetting resistance of thin-film silicate-filled PDMS composite membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 stripping performance as a function of operation time for the silicalite-filled PDMS-coated composite membranes and the PTFE porous membrane (Satorius Co, Germany, pore size 0.2 𝛍 m, thickness of 80 𝛍 m) is shown in Fig. 3. Operation conditions were as follow; CO2 loading 0.5, stripping temperature 80 ◦ C, and vacuum pressure 21.3, and 48.0 kPa (abs.). The porous PTFE membranes showed CO2 flux of 62 mol/(m 2 h) in the beginning while the silicalite-filled PDMS composite membranes showed CO2 flux of 54 mol/(m 2 h) during 2 h. In a previous paper (Simioni et al., 2011), porous PTFE and modified PES (polyethersulfone) membranes were reported to show CO2 desorption fluxes of 10-80 mol/(m 2 h) at 80-100 ◦ C for potassium carbonate solvent. When we compared CO2 fluxes of our two membranes with those of reference membranes, our composite membrane showed high CO2 fluxes comparable to those of reference membranes. This indicates the silicalite-filled PDMS materials are excellent coating materials to form thin dense layers with very low permeation resistance for vacuum stripping as expected because silicalite particle has many nano-sized pores and PDMS as a rubbery polymer also has high CO2 permeability. However, the CO2 flux of the PTFE membrane decreased drastically after 2 h and showed very low flux near 0 after 4 h while the silicalite-filled PDMS composite membrane maintained stable CO2 fluxes during 10 h of operation time without flux drop at low vacuum pressure of 21.3 kPa (abs.). The porous PTFE membrane is very hydrophobic and thus, it can be expected to have good flux stability in MEA solutions at 80 ◦ C. However, PTFE membranes showed the CO2 flux declining only after 2 h at the mild vacuum pressure condition (48.0 kPa (abs.)) due to pore wetting caused by sucking of MEA solution toward vacuum permeate side. The reason for the flux decline after pore wetting can be explained as follows; when the pores of the membrane are filled with aqueous solution, the CO2 mass transfer from bulk solution to permeate side in vacuum is strongly hindered due to thick solution-filled pore channels when compared with air-filled pore channels in nonwetting situation. This result confirms that, as we expected, the dense silicalite-filled PDMS-coated composite membranes show better resistance toward the common problem of pore wetting than the porous PTFE membrane and have lower membrane resistance for vacuum stripping.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Effect of temperatures on CO2 stripping
texts.text: 4.3. Effect of temperatures on CO2 stripping

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows the CO2 flux for the composite membranes at different temperatures in three 30 wt% amine solutions (MEA, DEA, and TEA) with CO2 loading of 0.5. The solution temperature was gradually increased by a thermostat from 25 to 80 ◦ C and the vacuum pressure for CO2 stripping was fixed at 8.0 kPa (abs.). It was found the CO2 fluxes increased for three amine solutions with increasing the desorption temperatures from 25 to 80 ◦ C. The CO2 fluxes for 30 wt% DEA and TEA solutions are higher than for 30 wt% MEA solution. The secondary DEA and tertiary TEA solutions easily regenerated CO2 at lower temperatures, unlike primary MEA solution. This behavior can be predicted by the reaction energy of 3 amines with CO2 because the reaction energy of TEA is reported to be 47 kJ/mol-CO2; DEA is 71 kJ/mol-CO2; MEA is 83 kJ/mol-CO2, respectively (Kim and Svendsen, 2011; Kwak et al., 2012a,b). With increasing the solution temperature from 25 to 80 ◦ C, more CO2 can be regenerated and transported to vacuum side in the order of TEA, DEA and TEA. TEA showed the highest CO2 flux at 40 ◦ C. The reaction energy behavior is expected to show similar tendency in other solution temperatures, so that TEA showed highest CO2 flux at each temperature. Also, these results can be well explained again by the increase of CO2 partial pressure observed with the increase in the temperatures of three amine solutions, as listed in Table 1. It means that the desorption temperature and the amine types are very important factors in the membrane vacuum stripping process of CO2.

texts.orig: 4.4. Effects of CO2 loadings on CO2 stripping
texts.text: 4.4. Effects of CO2 loadings on CO2 stripping

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effect of CO2 loading on the CO2 fluxes of composite membranes is presented in Table 5. The CO2 loadings of 30 wt% MEA solutions were carefully controlled from 0.5 to 0.7 and three vacuum pressures of 8.0, 21.3, and 34.7 kPa (abs.) were applied with the fixed temperature of 80 ◦ C. High CO2 fluxes are found at lower desorption pressures in the given range of CO2 loading due to the greater difference in the partial pressures of upstream and downstream sides. The CO2 desorption fluxes increased gradually when the CO2 loadings increased from 0.5 to 0.7. The effect of CO2 loading on CO2 flux is easily explained by the increase of CO2 partial pressure and CO2 concentration in the aqueous MEA solution. In other words, increasing the CO2 loading in the MEA solution increases the CO2 concentration in the solution through reversible equilibrium reaction, as shown in Eq. (2). Thus, the CO2 partial pressure also increases, as shown in Table 1, which enhances the driving forces of the CO2 permeation through the composite membranes.

texts.orig: 4.5. Effect of three aqueous amine solutions on CO2 stripping
texts.text: 4.5. Effect of three aqueous amine solutions on CO2 stripping

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows the CO2 fluxes for three different aqueous amine solutions with same CO2 loading of 0.7 for the composite membranes. As shown in Fig. 5(a-c), similar increasing trends for the CO2 fluxes were observed for three amine solutions (MEA, DEA and TEA) with decreasing vacuum pressures and increasing amine solution temperatures. Fig. 5(d) shows the CO2 fluxes for 30 wt% MEA, DEA, and TEA absorbents at the same operation temperature and vacuum pressure for performance comparison. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the CO2 stripping showed higher fluxes for TEA absorbent than for MEA and DEA. These results can be explained by the CO2 partial pressure of each amine solution at the same CO2 loading, as shown in Table 1. The CO2 partial pressures of 30 wt% DEA solutions at 25, 50, and 75 ◦ C were 6.8, 21.8, and 217.8 kPa, respectively, at similar CO2 loading of 0.5. However, the CO2 partial pressures of 30 wt% MEA aqueous solution at 25, 40 and 80 ◦ C were very low; 0.35, 2, and 40 kPa, respectively. In the case of 30 wt%, TEA, the CO2 partial pressure shows the highest value of 109 kPa. The driving forces in the DEA 30 wt% aqueous solutions were higher than those in the MEA 30 wt% solutions under the same CO2 loading conditions. The driving forces from the higher CO2 partial pressure result in higher CO2 stripping flux. This explains why the CO2 stripping flux with the 30 wt% aqueous TEA solution showed the highest values among the three amine solutions and MEA solutions showed the lowest CO2 flux.

texts.orig: In addition, the use of TEA or DEA aqueous solutions can be recommended in terms of energy consumption of CO2 stripping, because their CO2 stripping fluxes are much higher than those of the MEA solution. For example, CO2 stripping flux of over 120 mol/(m 2 h) was observed for DEA and TEA, whereas the CO2 flux with MEA was below 100 mol/(m 2 h) under the same operating conditions (vacuum pressure: 8.0 kPa (abs.), aqueous amine temperature: 80 ◦ C). However, the absorption rates of TEA and DEA are much lower than that of MEA and thus, a method to enhance the absorption rate of DEA and TEA solutions should be developed prior to the application into the amine absorption process.
texts.text: In addition, the use of TEA or DEA aqueous solutions can be recommended in terms of energy consumption of CO2 stripping, because their CO2 stripping fluxes are much higher than those of the MEA solution. For example, CO2 stripping flux of over 120 mol/(m 2 h) was observed for DEA and TEA, whereas the CO2 flux with MEA was below 100 mol/(m 2 h) under the same operating conditions (vacuum pressure: 8.0 kPa (abs.), aqueous amine temperature: 80 ◦ C). However, the absorption rates of TEA and DEA are much lower than that of MEA and thus, a method to enhance the absorption rate of DEA and TEA solutions should be developed prior to the application into the amine absorption process.
